text: The endoscopic view reveals the in situ colorectal anastomosis following the resection of rectal cancer. Fluorescence endoscopy is used to confirm the adequacy of perfusion at the anastomotic site.

The surgeon administers 5 ml of indocyanine green via a peripheral cannula. A near-infrared laparoscope is used through a proctoscope to visualize the colorectal anastomosis internally, while a second laparoscope provides an external view from the abdominal side.

The video shows excellent perfusion of the colorectal anastomosis, with fluorescence visible both proximal and distal to the circular stapled anastomosis. The serosal aspect of the bowel also demonstrates excellent perfusion, as observed through the second laparoscopic system.

This technology can be used both endoscopically and laparoscopically to detect fluorophore concentration and reassure the operating surgeon that there is an excellent perfusion and an absent tension on this colorectal anastomosis.
More information on near-infrared laparoscopy can be found elsewhere in this YouTube channel and in the special supplement of colorectal disease.
